questions.explanation: After VM termination, evidence may exist in CloudTrail/Activity logs (who created/modified/terminated the VM), VPC Flow Logs (network connections), storage snapshots (if configured), associated S3/Blob storage, and billing records. RAM and ephemeral storage are lost.

questions.question: A forensic examiner encounters an iPhone with a 6-digit passcode and cannot use Cellebrite for extraction. What alternative advanced technique might recover data?
options: Analyzing an unencrypted iTunes backup if one exists on a synced computer
Directly reading the NAND flash chips through chip-off on modern iPhones with Secure Enclave
Using brute force through the device interface without lockout protection
Extracting data through the Lightning port without any authentication
questions.explanation: If a forensic tool cannot bypass the device lock, checking for unencrypted iTunes/Finder backups on synced computers is a viable alternative. These backups may contain messages, photos, contacts, and app data. Cloud backups (iCloud) with proper legal authority are another option.

questions.question: What forensic evidence can be extracted from an Amazon Alexa or Google Home smart speaker?
options: Voice recordings, command history, and linked account data stored in the manufacturer's cloud
A complete recording of all conversations in the room at all times
Local storage containing years of offline voice recordings
Real-time video surveillance footage
questions.explanation: Smart speakers store voice command recordings, command history, and device interaction logs in the manufacturer's cloud (Amazon/Google). With proper legal authority, these can be obtained from the provider. The device itself stores minimal data locally.

questions.question: An investigator needs to perform a JTAG extraction on a mobile device. What does this technique involve?
options: Connecting to the device's JTAG test access ports on the circuit board to read memory directly
Using the device's USB port to download data through a standard connection
Extracting data wirelessly through Bluetooth
Removing the SIM card and reading it with a card reader
questions.explanation: JTAG (Joint Test Action Group) extraction connects to debug/test access ports (TAPs) on the device's circuit board to directly read the contents of flash memory. It bypasses the device's operating system and can work even when the device is damaged or locked.

questions.question: In a cloud forensics investigation, how should an investigator handle evidence collection from an AWS S3 bucket suspected of containing exfiltrated data?
options: Enable versioning/object lock, capture bucket policy and ACL, download objects with metadata, and preserve CloudTrail logs
Simply download all files and delete the bucket
Take a screenshot of the S3 console and move on
Ask AWS to provide a physical hard drive with the data
questions.explanation: Proper S3 forensic collection includes enabling versioning/object lock to prevent deletion, documenting bucket policies and ACLs, downloading objects while preserving metadata (timestamps, headers), and securing CloudTrail logs showing who accessed or modified the bucket.

questions.question: What is a chip-off extraction in mobile forensics and when is it used?
options: Physically removing flash memory chips from the device's circuit board and reading them with specialized equipment
Using software to copy data from the device over USB
Extracting the SIM card chip for analysis
Removing the device's battery to access hidden storage
questions.explanation: Chip-off extraction physically desolders flash memory chips from the circuit board and reads them using a chip programmer. It is a last-resort method used when the device is severely damaged or all other extraction methods have failed. It requires advanced skills and may destroy the device.

questions.question: An IoT device (smart thermostat) is suspected of being used as a pivot point in a network intrusion. What forensic approach should be taken?
options: Capture network traffic to/from the device, extract firmware for analysis, and examine the device's cloud API logs
Only examine the thermostat's temperature settings
Ignore the device since IoT devices cannot be used in attacks
Replace the device with a new one and analyze the old packaging
questions.explanation: IoT forensics for a compromised device involves capturing network traffic (may reveal C2 communication or lateral movement), extracting and analyzing firmware (may reveal modified code), examining cloud API logs, and checking for default credentials or known vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is an IMEI number and why is it forensically important?
options: A unique identifier for a mobile device that can link a specific phone to network activity
A password used to unlock the device
An encryption key stored on the SIM card
A serial number for the device's battery
questions.explanation: The IMEI (International Mobile Equipment Identity) is a unique 15-digit identifier assigned to every mobile phone. It can be used to track the device across cellular networks, identify stolen phones, and link a specific device to network activity in forensic investigations.

questions.question: Which cloud deployment model presents the MOST forensic challenges due to limited customer control?
options: Software as a Service (SaaS)
Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
Platform as a Service (PaaS)
On-premises deployment
questions.explanation: SaaS provides the least customer control and visibility into the underlying infrastructure. Forensic investigators are limited to user-level data and logs, with no access to OS, application, or infrastructure-level evidence controlled by the provider.

questions.question: When performing mobile forensics on an Android device, where are application databases typically stored?
options: /data/data/<package_name>/databases/
/sdcard/Android/databases/
/system/app/databases/
/proc/app/databases/
questions.explanation: Android application databases (SQLite) are typically stored in /data/data/<package_name>/databases/. This directory contains app-specific databases with user data, preferences, and cached content. Root access or a physical extraction is required to access this partition.

questions.question: A database administrator is suspected of manipulating financial records. Which database forensic artifact would provide the STRONGEST evidence of unauthorized data modification?
options: Database transaction logs showing the exact SQL statements, user ID, timestamp, and before/after values
Database performance metrics showing query execution times
Database backup file sizes from the last month
The database schema documentation
questions.explanation: Transaction logs (redo logs in Oracle, transaction log in SQL Server) record every modification with the exact SQL statement, the user/session that executed it, timestamp, and before/after values. This provides irrefutable evidence of what was changed, by whom, and when.

questions.question: In Google Cloud Platform forensics, which service provides detailed audit logs for resource access and modifications?
options: Cloud Audit Logs
Cloud Pub/Sub
Cloud Spanner
Cloud Dataflow
questions.explanation: GCP Cloud Audit Logs provide Admin Activity logs (resource configuration changes), Data Access logs (data read/write operations), and System Event logs. These are essential for investigating unauthorized access and data breaches in GCP environments.

questions.question: An investigator discovers that a suspect used anti-forensic techniques on their mobile device, including factory reset. What data might still be recoverable?
options: Data in areas not overwritten by the reset, cloud backups, carrier records, and app data synced to other devices
All data since factory resets don't actually delete anything
Nothing, factory reset securely overwrites all data on modern devices
Only the device's IMEI number
questions.explanation: While modern factory resets on encrypted devices are quite effective, residual data may exist in NAND flash areas not fully overwritten, cloud backups (Google/iCloud), carrier records (call/text logs), app data synced to other devices or cloud services, and social media accounts.
